description:
Hi, I have around 7+ years of experience in frontend development. My work mainly involves managing frontend workflows and ensuring the application behaves correctly while building responsive, accessible, and scalable web applications.

In a recent project, I worked directly with the client to understand requirements, coordinated with different teams, implemented features, and handled bug fixes and accessibility improvements. In another project, I translated design mockups into responsive UI, optimized performance, and enhanced the overall user experience while collaborating closely with backend and design teams.

I also focus on building reusable and maintainable UI components. For example, I developed a reusable table component used across multiple screens and a tokenizer-style search component for selecting guests and locations across different workflows.

Scalable means that the frontend code and components are designed so they can grow easily as the application adds more features, users, or screens without needing major rewrites.
- Reusable components: Components can be used in multiple places. Example: A table component used for guest lists, visits, reports, etc.
- Easy to extend: If a new feature is added, you can extend the existing component instead of rewriting it.
key_points: 7+ years of frontend development experience
Builds responsive, accessible, and scalable web applications
Works directly with clients and coordinates across teams
Translates design mockups into responsive UI
Focuses on reusable and maintainable UI components
Built shared table component and tokenizer-style search
Scalable: components grow with the app without rewrites

description: CORE is an internal desktop application used by Apple advisors to log and track customer issues coming from chat, email, and phone calls. Through CORE, advisors can document customer concerns, categorize each issue, and monitor the progress of tickets from creation to resolution.
key_points: Internal enterprise desktop application
Used by advisors for issue tracking
Supports chat, email, and phone channels
End-to-end ticket lifecycle management

description: One major feature I worked on was an AI/ML-based interaction summary feature, which was enabled for Prime users and the chat option. If the user is inactive and the chat has ended, then a summary is generated only when the chat has ended or the chat is inactive for a long time. A message is then sent to the backend, and based on the request header, we receive the summary from the backend API. Using the session ID, I displayed the summary in the IH module (Interaction History).
key_points: AI/ML-based feature
Triggered on chat end or long inactivity
Backend API communication using request headers
Session ID based summary mapping
Displayed in Interaction History module

description: VIMS (Visitor Management System) is internal app used by apple, but it also supports external use cases. It is used to schedule office visits and meetings, and to manage guest access across Apple buildings. VIMS acts as a central platform for organizing workplace visits efficiently. It is a multi-platform system accessed via Web, Mobile, iPad Kiosk, and Admin Portal interfaces, all of which share a single backend to ensure consistent data and access control.
key_points: Multi-platform system (Web, Mobile, Kiosk, Admin)
Single shared backend
Manages office visits and guest access
Supports both internal and external users

description: Feature-wise, I worked on visit scheduling with custom dates, location-based UI behavior, and notification-related flows. Most of my work involved handling complex UI state, reflecting backend validations clearly, and building reusable components like shared tables and tokenizer search to support these features across the application.
key_points: Complex UI state handling
Backend validation reflected in UI
Reusable shared components
Visit scheduling and notification flows

description:
In VIMS, one of the modules I worked on was the Recurring Visit Scheduling module, which allows users to create multiple visits at once instead of creating each visit manually.

The module supports three scheduling options:
1. Single-day scheduling: The user simply selects a date and time to create a visit.
2. Multi-day scheduling with two patterns:
   - Custom days: Users manually select specific days (e.g., Monday, Wednesday, Friday) within a selected date range.
   - Consecutive pattern: Users can create repeating visits over weeks, for example every Monday and Thursday for two weeks.

Once the recurrence pattern is selected, the system generates all the visit instances and displays them in a table so the user can review them before submitting.

One of the major functionalities in this module was handling overlapping visits, where only the first valid visit is accepted and any conflicting or duplicate visits are rejected.

Another major challenge was handling time zones, since Apple has buildings across many regions. A user could create a visit for a building in a different time zone, so the system had to ensure the visit time did not conflict. To solve this, the backend stored all visit times in UTC (Coordinated Universal Time), and on the frontend we converted the UTC time into the user's local time for display using the moment library. This approach ensured consistent time calculations across regions and prevented scheduling conflicts caused by timezone differences.
key_points: Recurring Visit Scheduling module in VIMS
Three scheduling options: single-day, custom days, consecutive pattern
Custom days: manually pick specific days (e.g. Mon, Wed, Fri) in a date range
Consecutive: repeat visits over weeks (e.g. every Mon & Thu for 2 weeks)
Generated visit instances shown in a review table before submission
Overlap handling: only first valid visit accepted; conflicts/duplicates rejected
Timezone challenge: Apple buildings across multiple regions
Backend stores all times in UTC; frontend converts to local time using moment.js
Prevented cross-timezone scheduling conflicts
